name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ قُلْ يَـٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥٣)
```

```english
Say [that Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah will forgive all sins. Indeed, He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (53)
```

```arabic
وَأَنِيبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (٥٤)
```

```english
Turn penitently to Him and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, whereupon you will not be helped. (54)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَحْسَنَ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (٥٥)
```

```english
Follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware.’ (55)
```

```arabic
أَن تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يَـٰحَسْرَتَىٰ عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطتُ فِى جَنۢبِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِن كُنتُ لَمِنَ ٱلسَّـٰخِرِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
Lest anyone should say, ‘Alas for my negligence in the vicinage of Allah! Indeed I was among those who ridiculed.’ (56)
```

```arabic
أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هَدَىٰنِى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٥٧)
```

```english
Or say, ‘Had Allah guided me I would have surely been among the Godwary!’ (57)
```

```arabic
أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى ٱلْعَذَابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Or say, when he sights the punishment, ‘If only there were a second chance for me, I would be among the virtuous!’ (58)
```

```arabic
بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتِى فَكَذَّبْتَ بِهَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٩)
```

```english
[They will be told,] ‘Yes, My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and acted arrogantly and you were among the faithless.’ (59)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُم مُّسْوَدَّةٌ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٦٠)
```

```english
On the Day of Resurrection you will see those who attributed lies to Allah with their faces blackened. Is not the [final] abode of the arrogant in hell? (60)
```

```arabic
وَيُنَجِّى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ بِمَفَازَتِهِمْ لَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلسُّوٓءُ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
Allah will deliver those who were Godwary with their salvation. No ill shall touch them, nor will they grieve. (61)
```

## (Commentary)

In these verses, He commands him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to invite them to Islam and to follow what Allah has sent down, and to warn them of the consequence of their extravagance (إسراف) against their own souls—namely, the regret and remorse on a Day when such feelings will not benefit them, given their arrogance (استكبار) toward the Truth in the world. Success and salvation on that Day are for the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the inner shield of protection against Allah's displeasure through the fulfillment of duties and avoidance of sins* — while the Fire and loss are for the disbelievers (الكافرين). Within the tone of these verses, there is a manifest tenderness and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ** "Say, [O Prophet, that Allah says,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah...’" (39:53). He commands him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call them on His behalf and to address them with the expression "O My servants" (يا عِبادِيَ). In this, there is a reminder of the proof (حجة) of Allah, the Glorified, for inviting them to His worship (عبادة), and an encouragement (ترغيب) for them to respond to the call. As for the reminder of the proof, it points to the fact that they are His servants and He is their Master (المولى); it is the right of the Master over His servant that the servant obeys and worships Him, so He has the right to call them to His obedience and worship. As for encouraging them to respond to the call, it lies in the attribution of them to Himself, the Exalted, which motivates them to cling to the hem of His mercy and forgiveness.

His saying: **الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ** "who have committed excesses against their own souls." Extravagance (الإسراف) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is exceeding the limit in every act a human performs, though it is most famous in spending. The verb here implies the meaning of a transgression (جناية) or something close to it, which is why it is followed by the preposition "against" (على). Extravagance against the soul is to transgress against it by committing sin (الذنب), whether it be polytheism (الشرك) or other major and minor sins, as the context suggests.

A group of commentators said: What is meant by "servants" (العباد) here are the believers (المؤمنون), as its usage when attributed to Allah, the Exalted, in the Quran is predominantly for them. Thus, the meaning of "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls" would be: "O sinning believers."

This is refuted by the fact that His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." until the end of the seven verses, possesses a single, connected context (سياق) that articulates their invitation. His saying at the conclusion of the verses: **بَلى قَدْ جاءَتْكَ آياتِي فَكَذَّبْتَ بِها وَاسْتَكْبَرْتَ** "‘Yes! My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and were arrogant...’" (39:59) is nearly explicit—or rather, it is explicit—that "servants" includes the polytheists (المشركين).

The instances in His speech, the Exalted, where the term "My servants" (عِبادِيَ) appears and refers specifically to the believers are about a dozen, all of which are surrounded by contextual clues (قرينة). It is not such that it automatically refers to the believers when used absolutely (الإطلاق), just as the instances where it is used absolutely to mean both polytheists and believers in His speech are also contextual.

In short, that "My servants" in this verse includes the polytheists is something that should not be doubted. The view that it refers to the polytheists specifically, in view of the context of the verses—as narrated from Ibn Abbas—is closer to acceptance than restricting it to the believers.

His saying: **لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ** "do not despair of the mercy of Allah." Despair (القنوط) is hopelessness (اليأس). What is meant by "mercy" (الرحمة), by the context of addressing the sinners and inviting them, is the mercy related to the Hereafter, rather than the more general mercy that encompasses both this world and the Hereafter. It is well known that what the sinners require from the aspects of the mercy of the Hereafter without intermediary is forgiveness (المغفرة). Thus, mercy here means forgiveness; for this reason, the prohibition of despairing of mercy is justified by His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً** "Indeed Allah forgives all sins."

In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) from the first person to the third person, where it is said: "Indeed Allah forgives" (إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ) instead of "Indeed I forgive." This is to indicate that He is Allah, to Whom belong the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), among which is that He is the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful. It is as if He is saying: "Do not despair of My mercy, for I am Allah; I forgive all sins because Allah is the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful."

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً** "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" is a justification for the prohibition of despair and an announcement that all sins are capable of being forgiven. Thus, forgiveness is general (عامة), but it requires a specific cause (سبب مخصص) and does not occur haphazardly (جزافاً). That which the Quran considers a cause for forgiveness are two things: intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* (1) — and repentance (التوبة). However, what is meant by "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" is not the forgiveness attained through intercession, because intercession does not reach

---
(1) The discussion regarding it has already passed in the discourses on intercession in the first volume of this book.

polytheism (الشرك) by the explicit text of the Quran in many verses. It has also passed that His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48) refers to intercession, whereas the current verse—namely "Indeed Allah forgives all sins"—concerns polytheism and all other sins.

Therefore, nothing remains except that what is meant is the forgiveness attained through repentance (التوبة). His speech, the Exalted, is explicit regarding the forgiveness of all sins, even polytheism, through repentance.

Furthermore, the seven verses—as you have known—constitute a single discourse with a connected context that prohibits despair—which is a preamble for what follows—and commands repentance, submission (الإسلام), and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). The first verse is not an independent, disconnected statement from what follows such that one could entertain the possibility that the generality of forgiveness therein is not restricted by repentance or any other assumed cause for forgiveness.

This verse—namely "Indeed Allah forgives all sins"—is a battlefield of opinions among them. A group went toward restricting the generality of forgiveness therein to polytheism and other major sins for which Allah has promised the Fire, while not restricting the generality by repentance; thus, they claim forgiveness only reaches minor sins.

Others went toward the absolute nature (إطلاق) of forgiveness, not restricting it by repentance or any other cause of forgiveness, except that they restricted it from polytheism due to the explicitness of His saying: "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him..." They concluded a generality of forgiveness even if there is no specific cause that gives preference to the forgiven sinner over others, such as repentance or intercession; this is haphazard forgiveness. They have argued for (1) this with several unsound points.

You are well aware that the subject of the verse is polytheism and all other sins. It is known from His speech, the Exalted, that polytheism is not forgiven except through repentance; thus, restricting the absolute nature of forgiveness in the verse to repentance is unavoidable.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ** "And turn to your Lord penitently and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, then you will not be helped" (39:54). This is coordinated (عطف) with His saying: "do not despair." Penitence (الإنابة) to Allah is returning to Him, which is repentance (التوبة). His saying: **إِلى رَبِّكُمْ** "to your Lord" is an instance of placing a manifest noun in place of a pronoun; the apparent requirement was to say "and turn to Him." The reason for this is to point to the justification, for the criterion in the worship of Allah, the Glorified, is the attribute of Lordship (ربوبية).

What is meant by submission (الإسلام) is surrendering to Allah and yielding to Him in what He wills. He said: **وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ** "and submit to Him" and did not say "and believe in Him" because what is mentioned before and after the verse is their arrogance (استكبار) against the Truth, and the opposite of that is submission.

His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ** "before the punishment overtakes you, then you will not be helped" is connected to "turn penitently" and "submit." What is meant by punishment (العذاب) is the punishment of the Hereafter, by the context of the following verses. It is possible, though remote, that it refers to any punishment with which repentance is no longer accepted, such as the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). The Exalted said: **فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ** "But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment—[this is] Allah’s precedent which has passed among His servants..." (40:85).

What is meant by His saying: **ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ** "then you will not be helped" is that forgiveness will not reach you in any way because its cause has not been realized; repentance is assumed to be non-existent, and intercession does not include polytheism.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "And follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware" (39:55). The address is general to both the believer and the disbeliever, like the preceding addresses, and the Quran has been sent down to both groups together.

In the verse is a command to follow the best of what has been sent down from Allah. It was said: What is meant is following the rulings (الأحكام) of the lawful and the prohibited, excluding the stories. It was said: Following what is commanded and forbidden, such as performing the obligatory and the recommended and avoiding the prohibited and the disliked, excluding the permissible. It was said: Following the firm duties (العزائم), which are the obligations and prohibitions. It was said: Following the abrogating (الناسخ) — *the later revelation that replaces a previous ruling* — rather than the abrogated (المنسوخ) — *the earlier revelation whose ruling has been replaced*. It was said: What was sent down is the genus of heavenly books, and the best of them is the Quran; thus, following the best of what was sent down is following the Quran.

In fairness, His saying in the previous verse: "and submit to Him" encompasses the content of each of these views; thus, restricting His saying: "And follow the best of what has been sent down to you" to any one of them is not free from redundant repetition without cause.

Perhaps what is meant by "the best of what has been sent down" are the addresses that point to the way of exercising the right of servitude (حق العبودية) in complying with the divine addresses regarding belief and action. These are like the addresses calling for the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, with total absorption, and toward His love, and toward godwariness (تقوى) as is His due, and toward the sincerity of religion (إخلاص الدين) for Him. Indeed, following these addresses gives man a "good life" (حياة طيبة), breathes into him the spirit of faith, rectifies his deeds, and enters him into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Exalted—and there is no dignity (كرامة) above that dignity.

His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware" is more suited to this meaning. For the call to an action by frightening one with the suddenness of deprivation and the unexpectedness of an obstacle usually occurs regarding matters where the one called is negligent, comforting himself with "soon" and "perhaps." This meaning is more closely related to the rectification of the inner self (الباطن) than the rectification of the outer self (الظاهر) and the performance of the mere shells of deeds. Close to this is His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ** "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life. And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart..." (8:24).

His saying, the Exalted: **أَنْ تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يا حَسْرَتى عَلى ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللهِ** "Lest anyone should say, ‘Alas for my negligence with regard to Allah...’" (39:56). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: Negligence (التفريط) is the neglect of what must be prioritized until its time passes. He said: Regret (التحسر) is the grief over what has passed its time because of its being cut off from him in a way that cannot be rectified. End quote. Al-Raghib said: The side (الجنب) is the limb. He said: Then it is used metaphorically for the direction adjacent to it, following their custom in using other limbs metaphorically for that, such as the right and the left. End quote. So "the side of Allah" (جنب الله) is His side and His direction; it is that which pertains to Him, the Exalted, regarding what is obligatory upon the servant to deal with. The instantiation (مصداق) of that is to worship Him alone and not to disobey Him. Negligence in the side of Allah is shortcoming in that.

His saying: **وَإِنْ كُنْتُ لَمِنَ السَّاخِرِينَ** "and indeed I was among those who ridiculed" (39:56). The word "that" (أَنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy particle. The ridiculers (الساخرين) is the active participle of "ridiculed" (سخر), meaning mocked (استهزاء).

The meaning of the verse is: We only address you with this discourse as a warning lest a soul among you should say: "Alas for my shortcoming in the side of Allah, and indeed I was among the mockers." The place of this saying is the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ اللهَ هَدانِي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ** "Or say, ‘Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious’" (39:57). The pronoun in "say" refers to the soul. What is meant by guidance (الهداية) is instruction and showing the way. The meaning is clear, and it is for the cutting off of excuses.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى الْعَذابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِي كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "Or say, when it sights the punishment, ‘If only I could have another turn, I would be among the virtuous’" (39:58). The word "if" (لو) is for wishing (التمني), and the "turn" (الكرة) is the return (الرجعة). The meaning is: Or a soul should say, wishing when it sees the punishment on the Day of Resurrection: "If only I had a return to the world, so I could be among the virtuous (المحسنين)."

His saying, the Exalted: **بَلى قَدْ جاءَتْكَ آياتِي فَكَذَّبْتَ بِها وَاسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ** "‘Yes! My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and were arrogant and you were among the faithless’" (39:59). This is a rebuttal to it and a response specifically to its second saying: "Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious." The place of the response is the Day of Resurrection, just as the place of the saying is that Day, and the context of the response bears witness to this.

A separation was made between its saying and the response by His saying: "Or say, when it sights..." etc., and He did not respond except to its saying: "Had Allah guided me..." etc.

The reason for the separation is that the three sayings narrated from it are arranged according to the order of their issuance from the criminals on the Day of Resurrection. When the Resurrection occurs and the criminals see that the Day is the Day of Recompense for deeds, and they had neglected them and their time had passed, they will feel regret for what they neglected and cry out with remorse over their negligence: "Alas for my negligence!" The Exalted said: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَتْهُمُ السَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً قالُوا يا حَسْرَتَنا عَلى ما فَرَّطْنا فِيها** "...until when the Hour overtakes them suddenly, they will say, ‘Alas for us, for what we neglected in it!’" (6:31).

Then, when they are reckoned and the God-conscious are ordered to enter Paradise and it is said: **وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** "‘Get apart today, O you guilty ones!’" (36:59), they will make excuses with their saying: "Had Allah guided me, I would surely have been among the God-conscious."

Then, when they are ordered to enter the Fire and are made to stand before it, then entered into it, they will wish for a return to the world to do good therein so they might be felicitous: "Or say, when it sights the punishment, ‘If only I could have another turn...’" The Exalted said: **وَلَوْ تَرى إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ فَقالُوا يا لَيْتَنا نُرَدُّ وَلا نُكَذِّبَ بِآياتِ رَبِّنا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "Were you to see when they are made to stand by the Fire, and they say, ‘Would that we were sent back, and then we would not deny the signs of our Lord and we would be among the believers!’" (6:27). And He said, narrating from them: **رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ** "‘Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to evil], we shall indeed be wrongdoers’" (23:107).

Thus, when He narrated the sayings according to the order between them, He took up the response. Had He delayed the saying responded to until it was adjacent to the response, or brought the response forward until it was adjacent to it, the order (النظم) would have been disrupted (1). He specifically addressed their second saying: "Had Allah guided me..." etc., with the response and refrained from responding to their first and third sayings because in the first there is mention of their mocking the Truth and its people, and in the third their wishing for a return to the world. Allah, the Glorified, rebukes these people on the Day of Resurrection and prevents them from speaking to Him, and does not respond to their speech, as indicated by His saying: **قالُوا رَبَّنا غَلَبَتْ عَلَيْنا شِقْوَتُنا وَكُنَّا قَوْماً ضالِّينَ رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَلا تُكَلِّمُونِ إِنَّهُ كانَ فَرِيقٌ مِنْ عِبادِي يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا آمَنَّا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّاحِمِينَ فَاتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا حَتَّى أَنْسَوْكُمْ ذِكْرِي وَكُنْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ تَضْحَكُونَ إِنِّي جَزَيْتُهُمُ الْيَوْمَ بِما صَبَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ** "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Our wretchedness overcame us, and we were a straying lot. Our Lord! Bring us out of it; then if we return [to evil], we shall indeed be wrongdoers.’ He will say, ‘Begone into it, and do not speak to Me! Indeed there was a part of My servants who would say, ‘‘Our Lord! We have believed. So forgive us, and have mercy on us, and You are the best of the merciful.’’ But you took them in ridicule until they made you forget My remembrance, and you used to laugh at them. Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience: they are the triumphant’" (23:106-111).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تَرَى الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللهِ وُجُوهُهُمْ مُسْوَدَّةٌ أَلَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ** "On the Day of Resurrection you will see those who lied about Allah with their faces blackened. Is not the abode of the arrogant in hell?" (39:60). Lying about Allah is the claim that He has a partner or that He has a son, and among it is innovation (البدعة) in religion.

The blackness of the face is a sign of humiliation (الذلة), which is the recompense for their arrogance; for this reason, He said: "Is not the abode of the arrogant in hell?"

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُنَجِّي اللهُ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا بِمَفازَتِهِمْ لا يَمَسُّهُمُ السُّوءُ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** "Allah will deliver those who were God-conscious with their triumph: no harm shall touch them, nor shall they grieve" (39:61). It appears that "triumph" (مفازة) is a mim-infinitive meaning success (الفوز), which is the attainment of what is desired. The preposition "with" (بـ) in "with their triumph" (بِمَفازَتِهِمْ) is for accompaniment or causality; thus, the success that Allah decrees for them today is the cause of their deliverance.

His saying: **لا يَمَسُّهُمُ** "no harm shall touch them..." etc., is an explanation of their deliverance, as if it were said: He delivers them such that no harm touches them from without, nor do they grieve within themselves.

The verse has a view toward His saying, the Exalted, at the conclusion of the verses of Surah al-Mu'minun quoted just now: "Indeed I have rewarded them today for their patience: they are the triumphant." Reflect and do not be heedless.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*, narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) that he said: "There is no verse in the Quran more encompassing than: **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ** (O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls...)."

I say: It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir from Ibn Sirin from him (peace be upon him). And there will come, Allah willing, in the commentary of Surah al-Layl, the narration from him (peace be upon him) that His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضى** "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased" (93:5) is more hopeful than this verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman* extracted from Thawban who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "I would not love to have the world and all that is in it in exchange for this verse: **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ** until the end of the verse." A man said: "O Messenger of Allah, what about one who has committed polytheism?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained silent, then said: "Except for one who has committed polytheism."

I say: There is something in this narration, for it has already passed that the subject of the verse is polytheism and that the verse is restricted by repentance.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Muslim extracted from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari who said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "Were it not that you commit sins, Allah would have created a creation that commits sins so that He might forgive them."

I say: The forgiveness mentioned in the hadith does not refuse being restricted by the causes of forgiveness such as repentance and intercession.

In the same source, it was said: This verse—meaning His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." etc.—was revealed regarding Wahshi, the killer of Hamzah, when he wanted to submit to Islam but feared that his repentance would not be accepted. When the verse was revealed, he submitted.

It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, is this for him specifically or for the Muslims generally?" He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) replied: "Rather, for the Muslims generally."

From the book *Sa‘d al-Su‘ud* by Ibn Tawus, quoting from the *Tafsir* of al-Kalbi: Wahshi and a group sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "Nothing prevents us from your religion except that we heard you reciting in your Book that whoever calls upon another god with Allah, kills a soul, and commits adultery will meet a penalty and abide forever in punishment—and we have done all of that." So He sent to them His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً** "excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously" (25:70). They said: "We fear that we might not act righteously."

So He sent to them: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48). They said: "We fear that we might not be included in the wish." So He sent to them: **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً** "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah; indeed Allah forgives all sins." So they came and submitted.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Wahshi, the killer of Hamzah: "Hide your face from me, for I cannot bear to look at you." He said: So he went to Syria and died in *al-Khamr*.

I say: Something similar was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through several chains, and in some of them, it is stated that His saying: "O My servants who have committed excesses..." etc., was revealed regarding him as in the report of *al-Majma‘* mentioned earlier. This is weakened by the fact that the Surah is Meccan while Wahshi submitted after the Hijrah. Furthermore, the apparent sense of the report is the lack of restriction of the absolute nature of forgiveness in the verse by repentance, and you have known that the context refuses this.

His saying: "he died in *al-Khamr*" (فمات في الخمر)—perhaps with a *fathah* on the *kha* and a *shaddah* on the *mim*—is a place in the outskirts of Medina. Perhaps it is a mistake of the people, and the correct name is Homs. Or perhaps what is meant is his death due to drinking wine (الخمر), for he was addicted to wine and was lashed for it more than once, then he stopped.

Know that there are many narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding the application (تطبيق) of these verses to their Shia and the application of "the side of Allah" (جنب الله) to them; all of these are of the nature of "flow" (الجري) — *the application of a verse to new instances that fit its general meaning* — rather than formal exegesis (التفسير). For this reason, we have omitted their inclusion here.
